key_findings: DNS + HTTP co-run is safe at full concurrency (250+75) consuming ~55% Oracle CPU; adding Enrich at 25 concurrent exceeds 32-core Oracle — DNS must reduce to 200 during Enrich steady state
Phase 1 (H0-H4) DNS-only validation is mandatory — activating HTTP before integrity gate is confirmed risks HTTP-DNS link corruption that propagates through the full graph
Controller RAM is the critical constraint at 16GB: Neo4j heap+cache requires 14-24GB; ClickHouse must move to Oracle if controller RAM is 16GB
ORS CPU governor (auto-reduce DNS by 25 at oracle_cpu > 80%) is required for Phase 3 steady state — manual CPU management is too slow for sustained 72h operation
DNS corpus completes well before H48 at sustained 200-250 concurrent (~14h for 10M corpus); HTTP is the long-tail lane requiring Phase 4 step-up to 100-150 concurrent to complete within 72h
Phase gate automation in AWSEM (next lane does not activate until ORS confirms gate) prevents manual timing errors during 72h unattended operation
Four-phase schedule provides 30-50% throughput increase over single-lane baseline while respecting Oracle CPU envelope and controller ingest capacity
Enrich lane ramps from 10 to 25 over 4h at activation — avoids CPU spike that would trigger ORS governor and reduce DNS prematurely

decisions.title: Phase 1: 4h DNS-only validation window
decisions.summary: No HTTP activation until Phase 1 gate passes: error_rate <= 2%, mismatch <= 0.5%, ORS green 30 min
decisions.rationale: Multi-lane activation before integrity validation risks HTTP-DNS link errors that corrupt graph topology; 4h gate window is cheap insurance against hours of bad data

decisions.title: DNS reduced to 200 concurrent in Phase 3 steady state
decisions.summary: DNS 250 + HTTP 75 + Enrich 25 exceeds 32-core Oracle; DNS compressed to 200 to stay within CPU envelope
decisions.rationale: 11% DNS throughput reduction is preferable to oracle CPU saturation causing thermal throttling and scan tool failures across all lanes

decisions.title: ORS CPU governor auto-reduces DNS concurrency
decisions.summary: oracle_cpu > 80% → reduce DNS by 25; oracle_cpu < 70% for 30 min → restore DNS by 25
decisions.rationale: 72h operation requires autonomous CPU management; manual adjustment is too slow and error-prone under sustained high-load conditions

decisions.title: ClickHouse relocates to Oracle if controller RAM <= 16GB
decisions.summary: Neo4j requires 14GB+ RAM on controller; ClickHouse moved to Oracle (30GB+ RAM available) via connection string update only
decisions.rationale: Sharing 16GB between Neo4j, ClickHouse, and Redis creates swap risk during ingest spikes; Oracle has sufficient RAM headroom at peak workload

decisions.title: HTTP step-up in Phase 4: 75 → 100 → 150
decisions.summary: As DNS corpus completes and Enrich winds down, Oracle CPU freed; HTTP steps up to clear HTTP corpus within 72h
decisions.rationale: HTTP is the long-tail lane; 75 concurrent cannot complete 2.5-4M HTTP targets in remaining Phase 4 time; step-up is necessary to meet 80% HTTP completion target

decisions.title: Enrich activates at 10 concurrent, ramps to 25 over 4h
decisions.summary: Gradual Enrich ramp prevents Oracle CPU spike at activation; ORS confirms CPU envelope before each increment
decisions.rationale: Nuclei/katana are CPU-heavy; sudden activation at 25 concurrent would spike CPU by ~16 cores simultaneously, triggering ORS governor and unnecessarily reducing DNS

phase_schedule.name: Scale-Up and Tail
objectives: Graph next-hop queries if T5 deployed

risk_model.title: Oracle CPU exceeds 85% in Phase 3
risk_model.mitigation: ORS CPU governor auto-reduces DNS; DNS is most compressible lane at Phase 3

risk_model.title: Controller RAM insufficient for Neo4j + ClickHouse
risk_model.mitigation: Move ClickHouse to Oracle; reduce Neo4j page cache to 8GB with SSD backing

risk_model.title: HTTP corpus not completable within 72h
risk_model.mitigation: HTTP step-up to 150 concurrent in Phase 4; HTTP completion target 80% (not 100%)

risk_model.title: Phase gate failure delays multi-lane activation
risk_model.mitigation: Gates are measurable; Phase 1 window builds high confidence before commit to Phase 2

risk_model.title: DLQ accumulation under network instability
risk_model.mitigation: Per-lane DLQ monitors; ORS alert at 500 items; DLQ TTL enforced

risk_model.title: Neo4j degrades at 10M+ node scale
risk_model.mitigation: Heap+page cache pre-configured; MERGE batch ≤ 500 ops; no full-graph scans during ingest

recommended_actions.action: Add oracle_cpu_utilization ORS monitor with DNS governor reflex before Phase 1
Assess controller RAM; if <= 16GB, relocate ClickHouse to Oracle before 72h start
Implement phase gate automation in AWSEM — next lane does not activate until ORS confirms gate
Export phase transition events and per-lane KPIs to Mattermost OPS on each phase change
Pre-configure Neo4j heap 6GB, page cache 8GB before Phase 1; verify startup < 5 min
Define per-lane concurrency config flags for runtime adjustment without restart
Record time_to_phase_n for each transition; compare against schedule for calibration
Set HTTP completion target to 80% of corpus by H72; schedule remainder in follow-on run

assumptions: A1: Oracle >= 32 CPU cores, >= 64GB RAM; CPU metrics available to ORS
A2: Controller >= 16GB RAM; 32GB recommended; SSD for Neo4j
A3: AWSEM supports concurrency adjustment on running lanes without restart
A4: ORS reads oracle_cpu_utilization as real-time signal
A5: Phase gate signals evaluated by ORS automatically; manual override available
A6: ClickHouse can relocate to Oracle via connection string update only
A7: DNS corpus (~10M) completable in ~14h at 700k/hr sustained; Phase 4 buffer handles retries and rate-limit pauses
